College version of American/Canadian gridiron football

This article is about gridiron football played at an amateur level. For other uses, see [College football (disambiguation)](/wiki/College_football_(disambiguation) "College football (disambiguation)").

"NCAA football" redirects here. For the video game series, see [*NCAA Football* (video game series)](/wiki/College_Football_(video_game_series) "College Football (video game series)").

Not to be confused with [College soccer](/wiki/College_soccer "College soccer").

**College football** is [gridiron football](/wiki/Gridiron_football "Gridiron football") that is played by teams of amateur [student-athletes](/wiki/Student_athlete "Student athlete") at universities and colleges. It was through collegiate competition that gridiron football [first gained popularity in the United States](/wiki/American_football_in_the_United_States "American football in the United States").

Like gridiron football generally, college football is most popular in the United States and Canada. While no single governing body exists for college football in the United States, most schools, especially those at the highest levels of play, are members of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA). In Canada, collegiate football competition is governed by [U Sports](/wiki/U_Sports "U Sports") for universities. The [Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Canadian_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "Canadian Collegiate Athletic Association") (for colleges) governs soccer and other sports but not gridiron football. Other countries, such as [Mexico](/wiki/Organizaci%C3%B3n_Nacional_Estudiantil_de_F%C3%BAtbol_Americano "Organización Nacional Estudiantil de Fútbol Americano"), [Japan](/wiki/American_football_in_Japan "American football in Japan") and [South Korea](/wiki/Korea_American_Football_Association "Korea American Football Association"), also host college football leagues with modest levels of support.

Unlike most other major [sports in North America](/wiki/Sports_in_North_America "Sports in North America"), no official [minor league](/wiki/Minor_league "Minor league") [farm organizations](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") exist for [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") or [Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football"). Therefore, college football is generally considered to be the second tier of American and Canadian football; ahead of [high school competition](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football"), but below [professional competition](/wiki/Professional_gridiron_football "Professional gridiron football"). In some parts of the United States, especially the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") and [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), college football is more popular than professional football.[[2]](#cite_note-2) For much of the 20th century, college football was generally considered to be more prestigious than professional football.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The overwhelming majority of professional football players in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and other leagues previously played college football. The [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") each spring sees 224 players selected and offered a contract to play in the league, with the vast majority coming from the NCAA. Other professional leagues, such as the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") (CFL) and [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2024) "United Football League (2024)") (UFL), hold their own drafts each year which also see primarily college players selected. Players who are not selected can still attempt to obtain a professional roster spot as an [undrafted free agent](/wiki/Free_agent#Undrafted "Free agent"). Despite these opportunities, only around 1.6% of NCAA college football players end up playing professionally in the NFL.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of American football](/wiki/History_of_American_football "History of American football") and [History of Canadian football](/wiki/History_of_Canadian_football "History of Canadian football")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may be [too long](/wiki/Help:Section#Section_size_policies "Help:Section") or excessively detailed.** Please consider summarizing the material. *(December 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Even after the emergence of the professional [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), college football has remained extremely popular throughout the U.S.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Although the college game has a much larger margin for talent than its pro counterpart, the sheer number of fans following major colleges provides a financial equalizer for the game, with Division I programs – the highest level – playing in huge stadiums, six of which have seating capacity exceeding 100,000 people.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In many cases, college stadiums employ bench-style seating, as opposed to individual seats with backs and arm rests (although many stadiums do have a small number of chair back seats in addition to the bench seating). This allows them to seat more fans in a given amount of space than the typical professional stadium, which tends to have more features and comforts for fans. Only three stadiums owned by U.S. colleges or universities, [L&N Stadium](/wiki/L%26N_Federal_Credit_Union_Stadium "L&N Federal Credit Union Stadium") at the [University of Louisville](/wiki/Louisville_Cardinals_football "Louisville Cardinals football"), [Center Parc Stadium](/wiki/Center_Parc_Stadium "Center Parc Stadium") at [Georgia State University](/wiki/Georgia_State_Panthers_football "Georgia State Panthers football") (which itself was a reconstruction of [Turner Field](/wiki/Turner_Field "Turner Field") which was a reconstruction of [Centennial Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Centennial_Olympic_Stadium "Centennial Olympic Stadium")), and [FAU Stadium](/wiki/FAU_Stadium "FAU Stadium") at [Florida Atlantic University](/wiki/Florida_Atlantic_Owls_football "Florida Atlantic Owls football"), consist entirely of chair back seating.

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

Main article: [Early history of American football](/wiki/Early_history_of_American_football "Early history of American football")

Modern North American football has its origins in various games, all known as "football", played at [public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(United_Kingdom) "Public school (United Kingdom)") in Great Britain in the mid-19th century. Early 19th century American college students played a disorganized game resembling medieval [mob football](/wiki/Medieval_football "Medieval football"). In 1827, a [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") tradition known as "Bloody Monday" began, which consisted of a mass ballgame between the freshman and sophomore classes. Violent games such as this were banned from college campuses around 1860.

By the 1840s, students at Britain's [Rugby School](/wiki/Rugby_School "Rugby School") were playing a game in which players were able to pick up the ball and run with it, a sport later known as [rugby football](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football"). The game was taken to Canada by British soldiers stationed there and was soon being played at Canadian colleges. The first documented gridiron football game was played at [University College](/wiki/University_College,_University_of_Toronto "University College, University of Toronto"), a college of the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), on November 9, 1861. In 1864, at [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College_(University_of_Toronto) "Trinity College (University of Toronto)"), also a college of the University of Toronto, F. Barlow Cumberland and Frederick A. Bethune devised rules based on rugby football.

The first game

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/The_first_game_rutgers_targum_nov_1869.jpg/250px-The_first_game_rutgers_targum_nov_1869.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_first_game_rutgers_targum_nov_1869.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Birthplace_of_College_football_plaque_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Birthplace_of_College_football_plaque_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birthplace_of_College_football_plaque_(cropped).jpg)

**Left**: "The Foot-Ball Match", a news article on the first college football game ever played, published in *[The Targum](/wiki/The_Daily_Targum "The Daily Targum")*, the [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") student newspaper, in November 1869. **Right**: A plaque on College Avenue on the campus of Rutgers University in [New Brunswick, New Jersey](/wiki/New_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "New Brunswick, New Jersey") commemorating the location where the first intercollegiate football game was played.

On November 6, 1869, [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_Scarlet_Knights_football "Rutgers Scarlet Knights football") faced [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_Tigers_football "Princeton Tigers football"), then known as the College of New Jersey, in [the first collegiate football game](/wiki/1869_New_Jersey_vs._Rutgers_football_game "1869 New Jersey vs. Rutgers football game"). The game more closely resembled soccer than rugby or gridiron football. It was played with a [round ball](/wiki/Ball_(association_football) "Ball (association football)"), and used a set of rules based on [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association")'s [first set of rules](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)").[[7]](#cite_note-PFRA1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-histfoot-8)[[9]](#cite_note-NFL1869-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

By 1873, representatives from Yale, Columbia, Princeton, and Rutgers met at the Fifth Avenue Hotel in New York City to codify the first set of intercollegiate football rules, based closely on association football (i.e., soccer). Before this meeting, each school had its own set of rules and games were usually played using the home team's own particular code.[[7]](#cite_note-PFRA1-7) However, the colleges coming to an agreement on soccer-like rules were unable to obtain the crucial support of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), which had been playing its own intramural football based on an informal style called the "[Boston game](/wiki/Boston_game "Boston game")".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/HarvardMcGill.jpg/250px-HarvardMcGill.jpg)](/wiki/File:HarvardMcGill.jpg)

The [McGill vs. Harvard football game](/wiki/1874_Harvard_vs._McGill_football_game "1874 Harvard vs. McGill football game") in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts") in 1874; Harvard won 3–0.

Unable to agree upon rules with American colleges, Harvard instead played [multiple games against McGill University in 1874](/wiki/1874_Harvard_vs._McGill_football_game "1874 Harvard vs. McGill football game"). In as much as Rugby football had been transplanted to Canada from England, the McGill team played under a set of rules which allowed a player to pick up the ball and run with it whenever he wished. Another rule, unique to McGill, was to count [tries](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)") (the act of grounding the football past the opposing team's goal line; there was no end zone during this time), as well as goals, in the scoring. In the Rugby rules of the time, a try only provided the attempt to kick a free goal from the field. If the kick was missed, the try did not score any points itself. Harvard players were enthusiastic about McGill's rules and quickly abandoned most of the "Boston game."

In 1875, Harvard played with adapted McGill rugby rules against Tufts, and then against its closest rival, Yale. Yale and Harvard agreed to play under a set of rules called the "Concessionary Rules", which involved Harvard conceding something to Yale's soccer and Yale conceding a great deal to Harvard's rugby. On November 13, 1875, Yale and Harvard played each other for the first time ever, where Harvard won 4–0. At the first [The Game](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry"), as the annual contest between Harvard and Yale came to be named, the future "father of American football" [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp") was among the 2000 spectators in attendance. Spectators from Princeton also carried the game back home, where it quickly became the most popular version of football.[[7]](#cite_note-PFRA1-7)

On November 23, 1876, representatives from Harvard, Yale, Princeton, and Columbia met at the [Massasoit House](/wiki/Massasoit_House "Massasoit House") hotel in [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts") to standardize a new code of rules based on the rugby game first introduced to Harvard by McGill University in 1874. Three of the schools—Harvard, Columbia, and Princeton—formed the Intercollegiate Football Association, as a result of the meeting. Yale initially refused to join this association because of a disagreement over the number of players to be allowed per team (relenting in 1879) and Rutgers were not invited to the meeting. The rules that they agreed upon were essentially those of [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") at the time with the exception that points be awarded for scoring a [try](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)"), not just the [conversion](/wiki/Try_(rugby) "Try (rugby)") afterwards ([extra point](/wiki/Conversion_(gridiron_football) "Conversion (gridiron football)")).[[12]](#cite_note-PFRA2-12)

Although American football was developed with input from McGill University, Canada had formed a separate amateur rugby league in 1873, the Foot Ball Association of Canada. The [Canadian Rugby Football Union](/wiki/Football_Canada "Football Canada"), founded in 1880, closely imitated American football rules, but interest in collegiate sports in Canada flagged over the succeeding decades and American football developed much more rapidly.

#### Walter Camp: father of American football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Walter Camp: father of American football")]

Main article: [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Walter_Camp_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_18048.jpg/250px-Walter_Camp_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_18048.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walter_Camp_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_18048.jpg)

[Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp"), the "Father of American Football", then the captain of the [Yale University football team](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs_football "Yale Bulldogs football"), in 1878

Walter Camp is widely considered to be the most important figure in the development of American football.[[8]](#cite_note-histfoot-8)[[9]](#cite_note-NFL1869-9)[[12]](#cite_note-PFRA2-12) Following the introduction of rugby-style rules to American football, Camp became a fixture at the Massasoit House conventions where rules were debated and changed. He reduced the number of players from 15 to 11 and established the [line of scrimmage](/wiki/Line_of_scrimmage "Line of scrimmage") and the [snap](/wiki/Snap_(football) "Snap (football)") from [center](/wiki/Center_(American_football) "Center (American football)") to [quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback"). Originally, the snap was executed with the foot of the center. Later changes made it possible to snap the ball with the hands, either through the air or by a direct hand-to-hand pass.[[12]](#cite_note-PFRA2-12)

Camp's new scrimmage rules revolutionized the game, though not always as intended. Princeton, in particular, used scrimmage play to slow the game, making incremental progress towards the end zone during each [down](/wiki/Down_(gridiron_football) "Down (gridiron football)"). Rather than increase scoring, which had been Camp's original intent, the rule was exploited to maintain control of the ball for the entire game, resulting in slow, unexciting contests. At the 1882 rules meeting, Camp proposed that a team be required to advance the ball a minimum of five yards within three downs. These down-and-distance rules, combined with the establishment of the line of scrimmage, transformed the game from a variation of rugby football into the distinct sport of American football.[[12]](#cite_note-PFRA2-12)

### Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Expansion")]

College football expanded greatly during the last two decades of the 19th century. Several major [rivalries](/wiki/List_of_NCAA_college_football_rivalry_games "List of NCAA college football rivalry games") date from this time period.

November 1890 was an active time in the sport. In [Baldwin City, Kansas](/wiki/Baldwin_City,_Kansas "Baldwin City, Kansas"), on November 22, 1890, college football was first played in the state of [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"). [Baker](/wiki/Baker_Wildcats_football "Baker Wildcats football") beat [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_football "Kansas Jayhawks football") 22–9.[[14]](#cite_note-kshs-14) On the 27th, [Vanderbilt](/wiki/Vanderbilt_Commodores_football "Vanderbilt Commodores football") played [Nashville](/wiki/University_of_Nashville "University of Nashville") (Peabody) at [Athletic Park](/wiki/Athletic_Park_(Nashville) "Athletic Park (Nashville)") and won 40–0. It was the first time organized football played in the state of [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee").[[15]](#cite_note-TNEncyc1890-15) The 29th also saw the first instance of the [Army–Navy Game](/wiki/Army%E2%80%93Navy_Game "Army–Navy Game"). [Navy](/wiki/Navy_Midshipmen_football "Navy Midshipmen football") won 24–0.

#### East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: East")]

Rutgers was first to extend the reach of the game. An intercollegiate game was first played in the state of New York when Rutgers played [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Lions_football "Columbia Lions football") on November 2, 1872. It was also the first scoreless tie in the history of the fledgling sport.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Yale football](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs_football "Yale Bulldogs football") starts the same year and has its first match against Columbia, the nearest college to play football. It took place at [Hamilton Park](/wiki/Hamilton_Park_(New_Haven) "Hamilton Park (New Haven)") in [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut") and was the first game in New England. The game was essentially soccer with 20-man sides, played on a field 400 by 250 feet. Yale wins 3–0, Tommy Sherman scoring the first goal and Lew Irwin the other two.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

After the first game against Harvard, Tufts took its squad to [Bates College](/wiki/Bates_College "Bates College") in [Lewiston, Maine](/wiki/Lewiston,_Maine "Lewiston, Maine") for the first football game played in [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This occurred on November 6, 1875.

[Penn](/wiki/Penn_Quakers_football "Penn Quakers football")'s Athletic Association was looking to pick "a twenty" to play a game of football against Columbia. This "twenty" never played Columbia, but did play twice against Princeton.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Princeton won both games 6 to 0. The first of these happened on November 11, 1876, in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and was the first intercollegiate game in the state of [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").

[Brown](/wiki/Brown_Bears_football "Brown Bears football") entered the intercollegiate game in 1878.[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Penn State](/wiki/Penn_State_Nittany_Lions_football "Penn State Nittany Lions football") played its first season in 1887,[[21]](#cite_note-nationalchamps.net-21) but had no head coach for their first five years, from 1887 to 1891.[[21]](#cite_note-nationalchamps.net-21) In 1891, the [Pennsylvania Intercollegiate Football Association](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Intercollegiate_Football_Association "Pennsylvania Intercollegiate Football Association") (PIFA) was formed. It consisted of [Bucknell University](/wiki/Bucknell_University "Bucknell University"), [Dickinson College](/wiki/Dickinson_College "Dickinson College"), [Franklin & Marshall College](/wiki/Franklin_%26_Marshall_College "Franklin & Marshall College"), [Haverford College](/wiki/Haverford_College "Haverford College"), Penn State, and [Swarthmore College](/wiki/Swarthmore_College "Swarthmore College"). [Lafayette College](/wiki/Lafayette_College "Lafayette College"), and [Lehigh University](/wiki/Lehigh_University "Lehigh University") were excluded because it was felt they would dominate the Association. Penn State won the championship with a 4–1–0 record. Bucknell's record was 3–1–1 (losing to Franklin & Marshall and tying Dickinson). The Association was dissolved prior to the 1892 season.[[21]](#cite_note-nationalchamps.net-21)

The first [nighttime football game](/wiki/1892_Wyoming_Seminary_vs._Mansfield_State_Normal_football_game "1892 Wyoming Seminary vs. Mansfield State Normal football game") was played in [Mansfield, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Mansfield,_Pennsylvania "Mansfield, Pennsylvania") on September 28, 1892, between [Mansfield State Normal](/wiki/Mansfield_University_of_Pennsylvania "Mansfield University of Pennsylvania") and [Wyoming Seminary](/wiki/Wyoming_Seminary "Wyoming Seminary") and ended at halftime in a 0–0 tie.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Army–Navy game of 1893 saw the first documented use of a [football helmet](/wiki/History_of_the_football_helmet "History of the football helmet") by a player in a game. [Joseph M. Reeves](/wiki/Joseph_M._Reeves "Joseph M. Reeves") had a crude leather helmet made by a shoemaker in [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis "Annapolis") and wore it in the game after being warned by his doctor that he risked death if he continued to play football after suffering an earlier kick to the head.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

#### Middle West

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Middle West")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Fielding_Yost-1902.jpg/250px-Fielding_Yost-1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fielding_Yost-1902.jpg)

A 1902 football game between the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football "Minnesota Golden Gophers football") and the [University of Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Wisconsin1903FootballTeam.jpg/250px-Wisconsin1903FootballTeam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin1903FootballTeam.jpg)

The [University of Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin_Badgers_football "Wisconsin Badgers football") football team in 1903

In 1879, the [University of Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") became the first school west of Pennsylvania to establish a college football team. On May 30, 1879, Michigan beat [Racine College](/wiki/Racine_College "Racine College") 1–0 in a game played in Chicago. The *Chicago Daily Tribune* called it "the first rugby-football game to be played west of the [Alleghenies](/wiki/Allegheny_Mountains "Allegheny Mountains")."[[24]](#cite_note-Will_Perry:_The_Wolverines:_A_Story_of_Michigan_Football_1974-24) Other Midwestern schools soon followed suit, including the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"), and the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football "Minnesota Golden Gophers football"). The first western team to travel east was the [1881 Michigan team](/wiki/1881_Michigan_Wolverines_football_team "1881 Michigan Wolverines football team"), which played at Harvard, Yale and Princeton.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The nation's first college football league, the Intercollegiate Conference of Faculty Representatives (also known as the Western Conference), a precursor to the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"), was founded in 1895.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Led by coach [Fielding H. Yost](/wiki/Fielding_H._Yost "Fielding H. Yost"), Michigan became the first "western" national power. From 1901 to 1905, Michigan had a 56-game undefeated streak that included a 1902 trip to play in the first college football [bowl game](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game"), which later became the [Rose Bowl Game](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_(game) "Rose Bowl (game)"). During this streak, Michigan scored 2,831 points while allowing only 40.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200016-28)

Organized intercollegiate football was first played in the state of [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") on September 30, 1882, when [Hamline](/wiki/Hamline_University "Hamline University") was convinced to play [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football "Minnesota Golden Gophers football"). Minnesota won 2 to 0.[[29]](#cite_note-Years-29) It was the first game west of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River").

November 30, 1905, saw [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Maroons_football "Chicago Maroons football") defeat Michigan 2 to 0. Dubbed "The First Greatest Game of the Century",[[30]](#cite_note-LA84-30) it broke Michigan's 56-game unbeaten streak and marked the end of the "Point-a-Minute" years.

#### South

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: South")]

[![An 1895 football game between Auburn and Georgia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/1895_Auburn_-_Georgia_football_game_at_Piedmont_Park_in_Atlanta_Georgia.jpg/330px-1895_Auburn_-_Georgia_football_game_at_Piedmont_Park_in_Atlanta_Georgia.jpg)](/wiki/File:1895_Auburn_-_Georgia_football_game_at_Piedmont_Park_in_Atlanta_Georgia.jpg)

An 1895 football game between [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn_University "Auburn University") and [Georgia](/wiki/University_of_Georgia "University of Georgia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Vmi_v_hokies_football_game_1894.jpg/250px-Vmi_v_hokies_football_game_1894.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vmi_v_hokies_football_game_1894.jpg)

An 1894 football game in [Staunton, Virginia](/wiki/Staunton,_Virginia "Staunton, Virginia") between [VMI](/wiki/VMI_Keydets_football "VMI Keydets football") and [Virginia Tech](/wiki/Virginia_Tech_Hokies_football "Virginia Tech Hokies football")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Sewanee_1899_Football_Team.jpg/250px-Sewanee_1899_Football_Team.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sewanee_1899_Football_Team.jpg)

[Sewanee](/wiki/Sewanee_Tigers_football "Sewanee Tigers football")'s 1899 "Iron Men"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Vanderbilt_football_1904.jpg/250px-Vanderbilt_football_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vanderbilt_football_1904.jpg)

The 1904 [Vanderbilt](/wiki/Vanderbilt_Commodores_football "Vanderbilt Commodores football") team in action; note the grid pattern on the field

Organized collegiate football was first played in the state of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") and the south on November 2, 1873, in [Lexington](/wiki/Lexington,_Virginia "Lexington, Virginia") between [Washington and Lee](/wiki/Washington_and_Lee_Generals_football "Washington and Lee Generals football") and [VMI](/wiki/1873_VMI_Keydets_football_team "1873 VMI Keydets football team"). Washington and Lee won 4–2.[[31]](#cite_note-generalssports.com-31) Some industrious students of the two schools organized a game for October 23, 1869, but it was rained out.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Students of the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia") were playing pickup games of the kicking-style of football as early as 1870, and some accounts even claim it organized a game against Washington and Lee College in 1871; but no record has been found of the score of this contest. Due to scantiness of records of the prior matches some will claim [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_football "Virginia Cavaliers football") v. Pantops Academy November 13, 1887, as the first game in Virginia.

On April 9, 1880, at [Stoll Field](/wiki/Stoll_Field/McLean_Stadium "Stoll Field/McLean Stadium"), [Transylvania University](/wiki/Transylvania_University "Transylvania University") (then called Kentucky University) beat [Centre College](/wiki/Centre_Colonels_football "Centre Colonels football") by the score of 13+3⁄4–0 in what is often considered the first recorded game played in the [South](/wiki/American_South "American South").[[33]](#cite_note-33) The first game of "scientific football" in the South was the first instance of the [Victory Bell](/wiki/Victory_Bell_(Duke%E2%80%93North_Carolina) "Victory Bell (Duke–North Carolina)") rivalry between [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_football "North Carolina Tar Heels football") and [Duke](/wiki/Duke_Blue_Devils_football "Duke Blue Devils football") (then known as Trinity College) held on [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)"), 1888, at the North Carolina State Fairgrounds in [Raleigh, North Carolina](/wiki/Raleigh,_North_Carolina "Raleigh, North Carolina").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

On November 13, 1887, the [Virginia Cavaliers](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_football "Virginia Cavaliers football") and Pantops Academy fought to a scoreless tie in the first organized football game in the state of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia").[[35]](#cite_note-35) Students at UVA were playing pickup games of the kicking-style of football as early as 1870, and some accounts even claim that some industrious ones organized a game against [Washington and Lee College](/wiki/Washington_and_Lee_Generals_football "Washington and Lee Generals football") in 1871, just two years after Rutgers and Princeton's historic first game in 1869. But no record has been found of the score of this contest. Washington and Lee also claims a 4 to 2 win over [VMI](/wiki/VMI_Keydets_football "VMI Keydets football") in 1873.[[31]](#cite_note-generalssports.com-31)

On October 18, 1888, the [Wake Forest Demon Deacons](/wiki/Wake_Forest_Demon_Deacons_football "Wake Forest Demon Deacons football") defeated the North Carolina Tar Heels 6 to 4 in the first intercollegiate game in the state of [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

On December 14, 1889, [Wofford](/wiki/Wofford_Terriers_football "Wofford Terriers football") defeated [Furman](/wiki/Furman_Paladins_football "Furman Paladins football") 5 to 1 in the first intercollegiate game in the state of [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina"). The game featured no uniforms, no positions, and the rules were formulated before the game.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

January 30, 1892, saw the first football game played in the [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") when the [Georgia Bulldogs](/wiki/1892_Georgia_Bulldogs_football_team "1892 Georgia Bulldogs football team") defeated [Mercer](/wiki/Mercer_Bears_football "Mercer Bears football") 50–0 at [Herty Field](/wiki/Herty_Field "Herty Field").

The beginnings of the contemporary [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference") and [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") start in 1894. The [Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Southern_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Association "Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Association") (SIAA) was founded on December 21, 1894, by [William Dudley](/wiki/William_Lofland_Dudley "William Lofland Dudley"), a chemistry professor at [Vanderbilt](/wiki/Vanderbilt_University "Vanderbilt University").[[38]](#cite_note-38) The original members were [Alabama](/wiki/University_of_Alabama "University of Alabama"), [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn_University "Auburn University"), [Georgia](/wiki/University_of_Georgia "University of Georgia"), [Georgia Tech](/wiki/Georgia_Institute_of_Technology "Georgia Institute of Technology"), [North Carolina](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill"), [Sewanee](/wiki/Sewanee:_The_University_of_the_South "Sewanee: The University of the South"), and Vanderbilt. [Clemson](/wiki/Clemson_University "Clemson University"), [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_University "Cumberland University"), [Kentucky](/wiki/University_of_Kentucky "University of Kentucky"), [LSU](/wiki/LSU_Tigers_football "LSU Tigers football"), [Mercer](/wiki/Mercer_University "Mercer University"), [Mississippi](/wiki/University_of_Mississippi "University of Mississippi"), [Mississippi A&M](/wiki/Mississippi_State_University "Mississippi State University") (Mississippi State), [Southwestern Presbyterian University](/wiki/Rhodes_College "Rhodes College"), [Tennessee](/wiki/University_of_Tennessee "University of Tennessee"), [Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin"), [Tulane](/wiki/Tulane_University "Tulane University"), and the [University of Nashville](/wiki/University_of_Nashville "University of Nashville") joined the following year in 1895 as invited charter members.[[39]](#cite_note-handbook-39) The conference was originally formed for "the development and purification of college athletics throughout the South".[[40]](#cite_note-SIAA-1895-40)

The first [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass") in football likely occurred on October 26, 1895, in a game between Georgia and [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_football "North Carolina Tar Heels football") when, out of desperation, the ball was thrown by the North Carolina back Joel Whitaker instead of punted and [George Stephens](/wiki/George_Stephens_(American_football) "George Stephens (American football)") caught the ball.[[41]](#cite_note-41) On November 9, 1895, [John Heisman](/wiki/John_Heisman "John Heisman") executed a hidden ball trick using quarterback [Reynolds Tichenor](/wiki/Reynolds_Tichenor "Reynolds Tichenor") to get [Auburn](/wiki/1895_Auburn_Tigers_football_team "1895 Auburn Tigers football team")'s only touchdown in a 6 to 9 loss to [Vanderbilt](/wiki/1895_Vanderbilt_Commodores_football_team "1895 Vanderbilt Commodores football team"). It was the first game in the south decided by a field goal.[[42]](#cite_note-slants-42) Heisman later used the trick against [Pop Warner](/wiki/Glenn_Warner "Glenn Warner")'s Georgia team. Warner picked up the trick and later used it at Cornell against Penn State in 1897.[[43]](#cite_note-43) He then used it in 1903 at Carlisle against Harvard and garnered national attention.

The [1899 Sewanee Tigers](/wiki/1899_Sewanee_Tigers_football_team "1899 Sewanee Tigers football team") are one of the all-time great teams of the early sport. The team went 12–0, outscoring opponents 322 to 10. Known as the "Iron Men", with just 13 men they had a six-day road trip with five shutout wins over [Texas A&M](/wiki/1899_Texas_A%26M_Aggies_football_team "1899 Texas A&M Aggies football team"); [Texas](/wiki/Texas_Longhorns_football "Texas Longhorns football"); [Tulane](/wiki/1899_Tulane_Green_Wave_football_team "1899 Tulane Green Wave football team"); [LSU](/wiki/1899_LSU_Tigers_football_team "1899 LSU Tigers football team"); and [Ole Miss](/wiki/Ole_Miss_Rebels_football "Ole Miss Rebels football"). It is recalled memorably with the phrase "... and on the seventh day they rested."[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Grantland Rice](/wiki/Grantland_Rice "Grantland Rice") called them "the most durable football team I ever saw."[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Organized intercollegiate football was first played in the state of Florida in 1901.[[47]](#cite_note-47) A 7-game series between intramural teams from Stetson and Forbes occurred in 1894. The first intercollegiate game between official varsity teams was played on November 22, 1901. Stetson beat Florida Agricultural College at Lake City, one of the four forerunners of the University of Florida, 6–0, in a game played as part of the Jacksonville Fair.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

On September 27, 1902, [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_football "Georgetown Hoyas football") beat Navy 4 to 0. It is claimed by Georgetown authorities as the game with the first ever "roving center" or [linebacker](/wiki/Linebacker "Linebacker") when [Percy Given](/wiki/Percy_Given "Percy Given") stood up, in contrast to the usual tale of [Germany Schulz](/wiki/Germany_Schulz "Germany Schulz").[[49]](#cite_note-49) The first linebacker in the South is often considered to be [Frank Juhan](/wiki/Frank_Juhan "Frank Juhan").

On [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)") 1903, a game was scheduled in [Montgomery, Alabama](/wiki/Montgomery,_Alabama "Montgomery, Alabama") between the best teams from each region of the [Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/Southern_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Association "Southern Intercollegiate Athletic Association") for an "SIAA championship game", pitting [Cumberland](/wiki/1903_Cumberland_Bulldogs_football_team "1903 Cumberland Bulldogs football team") against Heisman's [Clemson](/wiki/1903_Clemson_Tigers_football_team "1903 Clemson Tigers football team"). The game ended in an 11–11 tie causing many teams to claim the title. Heisman pressed hardest for Cumberland to get the claim of champion. It was his last game as Clemson head coach.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

1904 saw big coaching hires in the south: [Mike Donahue](/wiki/Mike_Donahue "Mike Donahue") at Auburn, [John Heisman](/wiki/John_Heisman "John Heisman") at Georgia Tech, and [Dan McGugin](/wiki/Dan_McGugin "Dan McGugin") at Vanderbilt were all hired that year. Both Donahue and McGugin just came from the north that year, Donahue from Yale and McGugin from Michigan, and were among the initial inductees of the [College Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame"). The undefeated [1904 Vanderbilt team](/wiki/1904_Vanderbilt_Commodores_football_team "1904 Vanderbilt Commodores football team") scored an average of 52.7 points per game, the most in college football that season, and allowed just four points.

#### Southwest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Southwest")]

The first college football game in [Oklahoma Territory](/wiki/Oklahoma_Territory "Oklahoma Territory") occurred on November 7, 1895, when the "Oklahoma City Terrors" defeated the [Oklahoma Sooners](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners_football "Oklahoma Sooners football") 34 to 0. The Terrors were a mix of Methodist college and high school students.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Sooners did not manage a single first down. By next season, Oklahoma coach [John A. Harts](/wiki/John_A._Harts "John A. Harts") had left to prospect for gold in the Arctic.[[52]](#cite_note-Ray_Soldan-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) Organized football was first played in the territory on November 29, 1894, between the Oklahoma City Terrors and Oklahoma City High School. The high school won 24 to 0.[[52]](#cite_note-Ray_Soldan-52)

#### Pacific Coast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Pacific Coast")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/1888-USC-football-team.gif/250px-1888-USC-football-team.gif)](/wiki/File:1888-USC-football-team.gif)

The first [USC football team](/wiki/USC_Trojans_football "USC Trojans football") in 1888; before they were nicknamed the "Trojans", they were known as the USC Methodists.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/1893_Stanford_American_football_team.jpg/250px-1893_Stanford_American_football_team.jpg)](/wiki/File:1893_Stanford_American_football_team.jpg)

The 1893 [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal_football "Stanford Cardinal football") football team

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Stanford_Field.png/250px-Stanford_Field.png)](/wiki/File:Stanford_Field.png)

The [Big Game](/wiki/Big_Game_(American_football) "Big Game (American football)") between Stanford and [Cal](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_football "California Golden Bears football") was played as rugby union from 1906 to 1914.

The [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") first fielded an American football team in 1888. Playing its first game on November 14 of that year against the Alliance Athletic Club, in which USC gained a 16–0 victory. Frank Suffel and [Henry H. Goddard](/wiki/Henry_H._Goddard "Henry H. Goddard") were playing coaches for the first team which was put together by quarterback Arthur Carroll; who in turn volunteered to make the pants for the team and later became a tailor.[[54]](#cite_note-USC2004MediaGuide201-54) USC faced its first collegiate opponent the following year in fall 1889, playing [St. Vincent's College](/wiki/Loyola_Marymount_Lions "Loyola Marymount Lions") to a 40–0 victory.[[54]](#cite_note-USC2004MediaGuide201-54) In 1893, USC joined the Intercollegiate Football Association of Southern California (the forerunner of the [SCIAC](/wiki/SCIAC "SCIAC")), which was composed of USC, [Occidental College](/wiki/Occidental_College "Occidental College"), [Throop Polytechnic Institute (Caltech)](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology"), and [Chaffey College](/wiki/Chaffey_College "Chaffey College"). [Pomona College](/wiki/Pomona_College "Pomona College") was invited to enter, but declined to do so. An invitation was also extended to [Los Angeles High School](/wiki/Los_Angeles_High_School "Los Angeles High School").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 1891, the first [Stanford football team](/wiki/1891_Stanford_football_team "1891 Stanford football team") was hastily organized and played a four-game season beginning in January 1892 with no official head coach. Following the season, Stanford captain John Whittemore wrote to [Yale](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs_football "Yale Bulldogs football") coach [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp") asking him to recommend a coach for Stanford. To Whittemore's surprise, Camp agreed to coach the team himself, on the condition that he finish the season at Yale first.[[56]](#cite_note-migdol-56) As a result of Camp's late arrival, Stanford played just three official games, against San Francisco's [Olympic Club](/wiki/Olympic_Club "Olympic Club") and rival [California](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_football "California Golden Bears football"). The team also played exhibition games against two Los Angeles area teams that Stanford does not include in official results.[[57]](#cite_note-lah-57)[[58]](#cite_note-cfbdw-58) Camp returned to the East Coast following the season, then returned to coach Stanford in [1894](/wiki/1894_Stanford_football_team "1894 Stanford football team") and [1895](/wiki/1895_Stanford_football_team "1895 Stanford football team").

On December 25, 1894, Amos Alonzo Stagg's [Chicago Maroons](/wiki/Chicago_Maroons_football "Chicago Maroons football") agreed to play Camp's [Stanford football team](/wiki/1894_Stanford_football_team "1894 Stanford football team") in San Francisco in the first postseason intersectional contest, foreshadowing the modern [bowl game](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game").[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) Future president [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover") was Stanford's student financial manager.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Chicago won 24 to 4.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Stanford won a rematch in Los Angeles on December 29 by 12 to 0.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The [Big Game](/wiki/Big_Game_(American_football) "Big Game (American football)") between Stanford and California is the oldest college football rivalry in the West. The first game was played on San Francisco's [Haight Street Grounds](/wiki/Haight_Street_Grounds "Haight Street Grounds") on March 19, 1892, with Stanford winning 14–10. The term "Big Game" was first used in 1900, when it was played on Thanksgiving Day in San Francisco. During that game, a large group of men and boys, who were observing from the roof of the nearby S.F. and Pacific Glass Works, fell into the fiery interior of the building when the roof collapsed, resulting in 13 dead and 78 injured.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) On December 4, 1900, the last victim of the disaster (Fred Lilly) died, bringing the death toll to 22; and, to this day, the "Thanksgiving Day Disaster" remains the deadliest accident to kill spectators at a U.S. sporting event.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The [University of Oregon](/wiki/University_of_Oregon "University of Oregon") began playing American football in 1894 and played its first game on March 24, 1894, defeating [Albany College](/wiki/Albany_College "Albany College") 44–3 under head coach [Cal Young](/wiki/Cal_Young "Cal Young").[[70]](#cite_note-timeline-70)[[71]](#cite_note-McCannOFB-71)[[72]](#cite_note-TODS13-72) Cal Young left after that first game and J.A. Church took over the coaching position in the fall for the rest of the season. Oregon finished the season with two additional losses and a tie, but went undefeated the following season, winning all four of its games under head coach Percy Benson.[[72]](#cite_note-TODS13-72)[[73]](#cite_note-CFBDW1894-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) In 1899, the Oregon football team left the state for the first time, playing the [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears") in [Berkeley, California](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California").[[70]](#cite_note-timeline-70)

American football at [Oregon State University](/wiki/Oregon_State_University "Oregon State University") started in 1893 shortly after athletics were initially authorized at the college. Athletics were banned at the school in May 1892, but when the strict school president, Benjamin Arnold, died, President John Bloss reversed the ban.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Bloss's son William started the first team, on which he served as both coach and quarterback.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The team's first game was an easy 63–0 defeat over the home team, Albany College.

In May 1900, Yost was hired as the football coach at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"),[[77]](#cite_note-77) and, after traveling home to West Virginia, he arrived in [Palo Alto, California](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California"), on August 21, 1900.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Yost led the 1900 Stanford team to a 7–2–1, outscoring opponents 154 to 20. The next year in 1901, Yost was hired by [Charles A. Baird](/wiki/Charles_A._Baird "Charles A. Baird") as the head football coach for the [Michigan Wolverines football](/wiki/Michigan_Wolverines_football "Michigan Wolverines football") team. On January 1, 1902, [Yost](/wiki/Fielding_H._Yost "Fielding H. Yost")'s dominating [1901 Michigan Wolverines football team](/wiki/1901_Michigan_Wolverines_football_team "1901 Michigan Wolverines football team") agreed to play a 3–1–2 team from Stanford University in the inaugural "Tournament East-West football game" what is now known as the *[Rose Bowl Game](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game")* by a score of 49–0 after Stanford captain Ralph Fisher requested to quit with eight minutes remaining.

The [1905 season](/wiki/1905_Stanford_football_team "1905 Stanford football team") marked the first meeting between Stanford and USC. Consequently, Stanford is USC's oldest existing rival.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The [Big Game](/wiki/Big_Game_(football) "Big Game (football)") between Stanford and [Cal](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_football "California Golden Bears football") on November 11, 1905, was the first played at [Stanford Field](/wiki/Stanford_Field "Stanford Field"), with Stanford winning 12–5.[[56]](#cite_note-migdol-56)

In 1906, citing concerns about the violence in American Football, universities on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"), led by [California](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") and [Stanford](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), replaced the sport with rugby union.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) At the time, the future of American football was very much in doubt and these schools believed that rugby union would eventually be adopted nationwide.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) Other schools followed suit and also made the switch included [Nevada](/wiki/University_of_Nevada,_Reno "University of Nevada, Reno"), [St. Mary's](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_College_of_California "Saint Mary's College of California"), [Santa Clara](/wiki/Santa_Clara_University "Santa Clara University"), and [USC](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") (in 1911).[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) However, due to the perception that West Coast football was inferior to the game played on the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") anyway, East Coast and Midwest teams shrugged off the loss of the teams and continued playing American football.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) With no nationwide movement, the available pool of rugby teams to play remained small.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) The schools scheduled games against local club teams and reached out to rugby union powers in Australia, New Zealand, and especially, due to its proximity, Canada. The annual [Big Game](/wiki/Big_Game_(American_football) "Big Game (American football)") between Stanford and California continued as rugby, with the winner invited by the [British Columbia Rugby Union](/wiki/British_Columbia_Rugby_Union "British Columbia Rugby Union") to a tournament in Vancouver over the Christmas holidays, with the winner of that tournament receiving the Cooper Keith Trophy.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80)[[81]](#cite_note-goldsmith-81)[[82]](#cite_note-bcru-82)

During 12 seasons of playing rugby union, Stanford was remarkably successful: the team had three undefeated seasons, three one-loss seasons, and an overall record of 94 wins, 20 losses, and 3 ties for a winning percentage of .816. However, after a few years, the school began to feel the isolation of its newly adopted sport, which was not spreading as many had hoped. Students and alumni began to clamor for a return to American football to allow wider intercollegiate competition.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) The pressure at rival California was stronger (especially as the school had not been as successful in the Big Game as they had hoped), and in 1915 California returned to American football. As reasons for the change, the school cited rule change back to American football, the overwhelming desire of students and supporters to play American football, interest in playing other East Coast and Midwest schools, and a patriotic desire to play an "American" game.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) California's return to American football increased the pressure on Stanford to also change back in order to maintain the rivalry. Stanford played its 1915, 1916, and 1917 "Big Games" as rugby union against [Santa Clara](/wiki/Santa_Clara_Broncos "Santa Clara Broncos") and California's football "Big Game" in those years was against [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Huskies_football "Washington Huskies football"), but both schools desired to restore the old traditions.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80) The onset of American involvement in World War I gave Stanford an out: In 1918, the Stanford campus was designated as the Students' Army Training Corps headquarters for all of California, [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), and [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), and the commanding officer Sam M. Parker decreed that American football was the appropriate athletic activity to train soldiers and rugby union was dropped.[[80]](#cite_note-park-80)

#### Mountain West

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Mountain West")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Colorado_football_team_1890.jpg/250px-Colorado_football_team_1890.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colorado_football_team_1890.jpg)

[Colorado](/wiki/Colorado_Buffaloes_football "Colorado Buffaloes football")'s first football team in 1890

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Utah_vs._Colorado_1916.jpg/250px-Utah_vs._Colorado_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Utah_vs._Colorado_1916.jpg)

Kickoff during the 1916 Colorado – [Utah](/wiki/Utah_Utes_football "Utah Utes football") game

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/1905_Utah_football_team.jpg/250px-1905_Utah_football_team.jpg)](/wiki/File:1905_Utah_football_team.jpg)

The 1905 Utah football team

The [University of Colorado](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_Boulder "University of Colorado Boulder") began playing American football in 1890. [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado_Buffaloes_football "Colorado Buffaloes football") found much success in its early years, winning eight Colorado Football Association Championships (1894–97, 1901–08).

The following was taken from the *Silver & Gold* newspaper of December 16, 1898. It was a recollection of the birth of Colorado football written by one of CU's original gridders, John C. Nixon, also the school's second captain. It appears here in its original form:

> At the beginning of the first semester in the fall of '90 the boys rooming at the dormitory on the campus of the U. of C. being afflicted with a super-abundance of penned up energy, or perhaps having recently drifted from under the parental wing and delighting in their newly found freedom, decided among other wild schemes, to form an athletic association. Messrs Carney, Whittaker, Layton and others, who at that time constituted a majority of the male population of the University, called a meeting of the campus boys in the old medical building. Nixon was elected president and Holden secretary of the association.
>
> It was voted that the officers constitute a committee to provide uniform suits in which to play what was called "association football". Suits of flannel were ultimately procured and paid for assessments on the members of the association and generous contributions from members of the faculty. ...
>
> The Athletic Association should now invigorate its base-ball and place it at par with its football team; and it certainly has the material with which to do it. The U of C should henceforth lead the state and possibly the west in athletic sports. ...
>
> The style of football playing has altered considerably; by the old rules, all men in front of the runner with the ball, were offside, consequently we could not send backs through and break the line ahead of the ball as is done at present. The notorious V was then in vogue, which gave a heavy team too much advantage. The mass plays being now barred, skill on the football field is more in demand than mere weight and strength.

— John C. Nixon, *Silver & Gold*, December 16, 1898[[83]](#cite_note-83)

In 1909, the [Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_Athletic_Conference "Rocky Mountain Athletic Conference") was founded, featuring four members: Colorado, [Colorado College](/wiki/Colorado_College "Colorado College"), [Colorado School of Mines](/wiki/Colorado_School_of_Mines "Colorado School of Mines"), and [Colorado Agricultural College](/wiki/Colorado_State_University "Colorado State University"). The [University of Denver](/wiki/University_of_Denver "University of Denver") and the [University of Utah](/wiki/University_of_Utah "University of Utah") joined the RMAC in 1910. For its first thirty years, the RMAC was considered a major conference equivalent to today's Division I, before 7 larger members left and formed the [Mountain States Conference](/wiki/Mountain_States_Conference "Mountain States Conference") (also called the Skyline Conference).

### Violence, formation of NCAA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Violence, formation of NCAA")]

College football increased in popularity through the remainder of the 19th and early 20th century. It also became increasingly violent. Between 1890 and 1905, 330 college athletes died as a direct result of injuries sustained on the football field. These deaths could be attributed to the mass formations and gang tackling that characterized the sport in its early years.

> No sport is wholesome in which ungenerous or mean acts which easily escape detection contribute to victory.

[Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot"), President of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") (1869–1909) opposing football in 1905.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

The 1894 Harvard–Yale game, known as the "Hampden Park Blood Bath", resulted in crippling injuries for four players; the contest was suspended until 1897. The annual Army–Navy game was suspended from 1894 to 1898 for similar reasons.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200016–18-85) One of the major problems was the popularity of mass-formations like the [flying wedge](/wiki/Flying_wedge "Flying wedge"), in which a large number of offensive players charged as a unit against a similarly arranged defense. The resultant collisions often led to serious injuries and sometimes even death.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett197620-86) Georgia fullback [Richard Von Albade Gammon](/wiki/Richard_Von_Albade_Gammon "Richard Von Albade Gammon") notably died on the field from concussions received against Virginia in 1897, causing Georgia, Georgia Tech, and Mercer to suspend their football programs.

The situation came to a head in 1905 when there were 19 fatalities nationwide. President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") reportedly threatened to shut down the game if drastic changes were not made.[[87]](#cite_note-87) However, the threat by Roosevelt to eliminate football is disputed by sports historians. What is absolutely certain is that on October 9, 1905, Roosevelt held a meeting of football representatives from [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), [Yale](/wiki/Yale "Yale"), and [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"). Though he lectured on eliminating and reducing injuries, he never threatened to ban football. He also lacked the authority to abolish football and was, in fact, actually a fan of the sport and wanted to preserve it. The President's sons were also playing football at the college and [secondary levels](/wiki/High_school_football "High school football") at the time.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

Meanwhile, [John H. Outland](/wiki/John_H._Outland "John H. Outland") held an [experimental game](/wiki/1905_Fairmount_vs._Washburn_football_game "1905 Fairmount vs. Washburn football game") in [Wichita, Kansas](/wiki/Wichita,_Kansas "Wichita, Kansas") that reduced the number of scrimmage plays to earn a first down from four to three in an attempt to reduce injuries.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* reported an increase in punts and considered the game much safer than regular play but that the new rule was not "conducive to the sport".[[90]](#cite_note-90) In 1906, President Roosevelt organized a meeting among thirteen school leaders at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") to find solutions to make the sport safer for the athletes. Because the college officials could not agree upon a change in rules, it was decided over the course of several subsequent meetings that an external governing body should be responsible. Finally, on December 28, 1905, 62 schools met in New York City to discuss rule changes to make the game safer. As a result of this meeting, the Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States was formed in 1906. The IAAUS was the original rule-making body of college football, but would go on to sponsor championships in other sports. The IAAUS would get its current name of [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) in 1910,[[91]](#cite_note-Smith2000-91) and still sets rules governing the sport.[[91]](#cite_note-Smith2000-91)[[92]](#cite_note-NCAA-92)

The rules committee considered widening the playing field to "open up" the game, but [Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium") (the first large permanent football stadium) had recently been built at great expense; it would be rendered useless by a wider field. The rules committee legalized the [forward pass](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass") instead. Though it was underused for years, this proved to be one of the most important rule changes in the establishment of the modern game.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200018-93) Another rule change banned "mass momentum" plays (many of which, like the infamous "flying wedge", were sometimes literally deadly).

### Modernization and innovation (1906–1930)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Modernization and innovation (1906–1930)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/RobinsonThrowing.jpg/250px-RobinsonThrowing.jpg)](/wiki/File:RobinsonThrowing.jpg)

A 1906 *[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")* photograph of [Brad Robinson](/wiki/Bradbury_Robinson "Bradbury Robinson"), who threw the first legal forward pass and was the sport's first [triple threat](/wiki/Triple_threat_man "Triple threat man")

As a result of the 1905–1906 reforms, mass formation plays became illegal and [forward passes](/wiki/Forward_pass "Forward pass") legal. [Bradbury Robinson](/wiki/Bradbury_Robinson "Bradbury Robinson"), playing for visionary coach [Eddie Cochems](/wiki/Eddie_Cochems "Eddie Cochems") at [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)"), threw the first legal pass in a September 5, 1906, game against [Carroll College](/wiki/Carroll_University "Carroll University") at [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha,_Wisconsin "Waukesha, Wisconsin"). Other important changes, formally adopted in 1910, were the requirements that at least seven offensive players be on the line of scrimmage at the time of the snap, that there be no pushing or pulling, and that interlocking interference (arms linked or hands on belts and uniforms) was not allowed. These changes greatly reduced the potential for collision injuries.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Several coaches emerged who took advantage of these sweeping changes. [Amos Alonzo Stagg](/wiki/Amos_Alonzo_Stagg "Amos Alonzo Stagg") introduced such innovations as the [huddle](/wiki/Huddle "Huddle"), the [tackling dummy](/wiki/Tackling_dummy "Tackling dummy"), and the pre-snap shift.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200017-95) Other coaches, such as [Pop Warner](/wiki/Pop_Warner "Pop Warner") and [Knute Rockne](/wiki/Knute_Rockne "Knute Rockne"), introduced new strategies that still remain part of the game.

Besides these coaching innovations, several rules changes during the first third of the 20th century had a profound impact on the game, mostly in opening up the passing game. In 1914, the first roughing-the-passer penalty was implemented. In 1918, the rules on eligible receivers were loosened to allow eligible players to catch the ball anywhere on the field—previously strict rules were in place allowing passes to only certain areas of the field.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200022-96) Scoring rules also changed during this time: field goals were lowered to three points in 1909[[9]](#cite_note-NFL1869-9) and touchdowns raised to six points in 1912.[[97]](#cite_note-NFL1911-97)

Star players that emerged in the early 20th century include [Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe"), [Red Grange](/wiki/Red_Grange "Red Grange"), and [Bronko Nagurski](/wiki/Bronko_Nagurski "Bronko Nagurski"); these three made the transition to the fledgling NFL and helped turn it into a successful league. Sportswriter [Grantland Rice](/wiki/Grantland_Rice "Grantland Rice") helped popularize the sport with his poetic descriptions of games and colorful nicknames for the game's biggest players, including Notre Dame's "[Four Horsemen](/wiki/Four_Horsemen_(American_football) "Four Horsemen (American football)")" backfield and [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_Rams_football "Fordham Rams football")'s linemen, known as the "[Seven Blocks of Granite](/wiki/Seven_Blocks_of_Granite "Seven Blocks of Granite")".[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200024-98)

In 1907 at [Champaign, Illinois](/wiki/Champaign,_Illinois "Champaign, Illinois") Chicago and [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_Fighting_Illini_football "Illinois Fighting Illini football") played in the first game to have a halftime show featuring a [marching band](/wiki/Marching_band "Marching band").[[99]](#cite_note-99) Chicago won 42–6. On [November 25, 1911](/wiki/1911_Kansas_vs._Missouri_football_game "1911 Kansas vs. Missouri football game") [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_football "Kansas Jayhawks football") played at [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri_Tigers_football "Missouri Tigers football") in the first [homecoming](/wiki/Homecoming "Homecoming") football game.[[100]](#cite_note-100) The game was "broadcast" play-by-play over telegraph to at least 1,000 fans in [Lawrence, Kansas](/wiki/Lawrence,_Kansas "Lawrence, Kansas").[[101]](#cite_note-mech-101) It ended in a 3–3 tie. The game between [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia_Mountaineers_football "West Virginia Mountaineers football") and [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Panthers_football "Pittsburgh Panthers football") on October 8, 1921, saw the first live radio broadcast of a college football game when Harold W. Arlin announced that year's [Backyard Brawl](/wiki/Backyard_Brawl "Backyard Brawl") played at [Forbes Field](/wiki/Forbes_Field "Forbes Field") on [KDKA](/wiki/KDKA_(AM) "KDKA (AM)"). Pitt won 21–13.[[102]](#cite_note-102) On October 28, 1922, Princeton and Chicago played the first game to be nationally broadcast on radio. Princeton won 21–18 in a hotly contested game which had Princeton dubbed the "Team of Destiny".[[103]](#cite_note-103)

#### Rise of the South

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Rise of the South")]

One publication claims "The first scouting done in the South was in 1905, when [Dan McGugin](/wiki/Dan_McGugin "Dan McGugin") and Captain [Innis Brown](/wiki/Innis_Brown "Innis Brown"), of Vanderbilt went to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") to see [Sewanee](/wiki/Sewanee_Tigers_football "Sewanee Tigers football") play [Georgia Tech](/wiki/Georgia_Tech_Yellow_Jackets_football "Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football")."[[104]](#cite_note-104) [Fuzzy Woodruff](/wiki/Fuzzy_Woodruff "Fuzzy Woodruff") claims [Davidson](/wiki/Davidson_Wildcats_football "Davidson Wildcats football") was the first in the south to throw a legal forward pass in 1906. The following season saw [Vanderbilt](/wiki/1907_Vanderbilt_Commodores_football_team "1907 Vanderbilt Commodores football team") execute a [double pass play](/wiki/Trick_play#double_pass "Trick play") to set up the touchdown that beat Sewanee in a meeting of the unbeaten for the SIAA championship. [Grantland Rice](/wiki/Grantland_Rice "Grantland Rice") cited this event as the greatest thrill he ever witnessed in his years of watching sports.[[105]](#cite_note-105) Vanderbilt coach Dan McGugin in *Spalding's Football Guide*'s summation of the season in the SIAA wrote "The standing. First, Vanderbilt; second, Sewanee, a might good second;" and that [Aubrey Lanier](/wiki/Aubrey_Lanier "Aubrey Lanier") "came near winning the Vanderbilt game by his brilliant dashes after receiving punts."[[106]](#cite_note-106) [Bob Blake](/wiki/Bob_Blake_(American_football) "Bob Blake (American football)") threw the final pass to center [Stein Stone](/wiki/Stein_Stone "Stein Stone"), catching it near the goal among defenders. [Honus Craig](/wiki/Honus_Craig "Honus Craig") then ran in the winning touchdown.

##### Heisman shift

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Heisman shift")]

Using the "[jump shift](/wiki/Jump_shift "Jump shift")" offense, [John Heisman](/wiki/John_Heisman "John Heisman")'s [Georgia Tech Golden Tornado](/wiki/Georgia_Tech_Yellow_Jackets_football "Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football") won [222 to 0](/wiki/1916_Cumberland_vs._Georgia_Tech_football_game "1916 Cumberland vs. Georgia Tech football game") over [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland_University "Cumberland University") on October 7, 1916, at [Grant Field](/wiki/Grant_Field "Grant Field") in the most lopsided victory in college football history.[[107]](#cite_note-pbp-107) Tech went on a 33-game winning streak during this period. The [1917 team](/wiki/1917_Georgia_Tech_Yellow_Jackets_football_team "1917 Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football team") was the first [national champion](/wiki/Mythical_national_championship "Mythical national championship") from the [South](/wiki/American_South "American South"), led by a powerful backfield. It also had the first two players from the [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") selected first-team All-American in [Walker Carpenter](/wiki/Walker_Carpenter "Walker Carpenter") and [Everett Strupper](/wiki/Everett_Strupper "Everett Strupper"). [Pop Warner](/wiki/Glenn_Warner "Glenn Warner")'s [Pittsburgh Panthers](/wiki/1917_Pittsburgh_Panthers_football_team "1917 Pittsburgh Panthers football team") were also undefeated, but declined a challenge by Heisman to a game. When Heisman left Tech after 1919, his shift was still employed by protégé [William Alexander](/wiki/William_Alexander_(coach) "William Alexander (coach)").

##### Notable intersectional games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Notable intersectional games")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/DaviesPittGT1918.jpg/250px-DaviesPittGT1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:DaviesPittGT1918.jpg)

[Tom Davies](/wiki/Tom_Davies_(American_football) "Tom Davies (American football)") runs against undefeated and unscored upon [Georgia Tech](/wiki/1918_Georgia_Tech_Yellow_Jackets_football_team "1918 Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football team") in the 1918 game at [Forbes Field](/wiki/Forbes_Field "Forbes Field")

In 1906, Vanderbilt defeated [Carlisle](/wiki/Carlisle_Indians_football "Carlisle Indians football") 4 to 0, the result of a Bob Blake field goal.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-Best_Eleven-109) In 1907 Vanderbilt fought Navy to a 6 to 6 tie. In 1910 Vanderbilt held defending national champion Yale to a scoreless tie.[[109]](#cite_note-Best_Eleven-109)

Helping Georgia Tech's claim to a title in 1917, the [Auburn Tigers](/wiki/1917_Auburn_Tigers_football_team "1917 Auburn Tigers football team") held undefeated, [Chic Harley](/wiki/Chic_Harley "Chic Harley")-led Big Ten champion [Ohio State](/wiki/1917_Ohio_State_Buckeyes_football_team "1917 Ohio State Buckeyes football team") to a scoreless tie the week before Georgia Tech beat the Tigers 68 to 7. The next season, with many players gone due to World War I, a game was finally scheduled at [Forbes Field](/wiki/Forbes_Field "Forbes Field") with [Pittsburgh](/wiki/1918_Pittsburgh_Panthers_football_team "1918 Pittsburgh Panthers football team"). The Panthers, led by freshman [Tom Davies](/wiki/Tom_Davies_(American_football) "Tom Davies (American football)"), defeated [Georgia Tech](/wiki/1918_Georgia_Tech_Yellow_Jackets_football_team "1918 Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets football team") 32 to 0. Tech center [Bum Day](/wiki/Bum_Day "Bum Day") was the first player on a Southern team ever selected first-team All-American by [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp").

1917 saw the rise of another Southern team in [Centre](/wiki/Centre_Colonels_football "Centre Colonels football") of [Danville, Kentucky](/wiki/Danville,_Kentucky "Danville, Kentucky"). In 1921 [Bo McMillin](/wiki/Bo_McMillin "Bo McMillin")-led [Centre](/wiki/1921_Centre_Praying_Colonels_football_team "1921 Centre Praying Colonels football team") upset defending national champion Harvard [6 to 0](/wiki/1921_Centre_vs._Harvard_football_game "1921 Centre vs. Harvard football game") in what is widely considered one of the greatest upsets in college football history. The next year Vanderbilt fought Michigan to a [scoreless tie](/wiki/1922_Michigan_vs._Vanderbilt_football_game "1922 Michigan vs. Vanderbilt football game") at the inaugural game at [Dudley Field](/wiki/Dudley_Field "Dudley Field") (now Vanderbilt Stadium), the first stadium in the South made exclusively for college football. Michigan coach [Fielding Yost](/wiki/Fielding_Yost "Fielding Yost") and Vanderbilt coach [Dan McGugin](/wiki/Dan_McGugin "Dan McGugin") were brothers-in-law, and the latter the protégé of the former. The game featured the season's two best defenses and included a goal line stand by Vanderbilt to preserve the tie. Its result was "a great surprise to the sporting world".[[110]](#cite_note-110) Commodore fans celebrated by throwing some 3,000 seat cushions onto the field. The game features prominently in Vanderbilt's history.[[111]](#cite_note-Traughber-111) That same year, Alabama upset [Penn](/wiki/Penn_Quakers_football "Penn Quakers football") 9 to 7.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

Vanderbilt's line coach then was [Wallace Wade](/wiki/Wallace_Wade "Wallace Wade"), who coached [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama_Crimson_Tide_football "Alabama Crimson Tide football") to the South's first [Rose Bowl](/wiki/1926_Rose_Bowl "1926 Rose Bowl") victory in 1925. This game is commonly referred to as "the game that changed the south".[[113]](#cite_note-113) Wade followed up the next season with an undefeated record and [Rose Bowl](/wiki/1927_Rose_Bowl "1927 Rose Bowl") tie. Georgia's 1927 "[dream and wonder team](/wiki/1927_Georgia_Bulldogs_football_team "1927 Georgia Bulldogs football team")" [defeated](/wiki/1927_Georgia_vs._Yale_football_game "1927 Georgia vs. Yale football game") [Yale](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs_football "Yale Bulldogs football") for the first time. Georgia Tech, led by Heisman protégé [William Alexander](/wiki/William_Alexander_(coach) "William Alexander (coach)"), gave the dream and wonder team its only loss, and the next year were national and [Rose Bowl](/wiki/1929_Rose_Bowl "1929 Rose Bowl") champions. The Rose Bowl included [Roy Riegels](/wiki/Roy_Riegels "Roy Riegels")' wrong-way run. On October 12, 1929, Yale lost to Georgia in [Sanford Stadium](/wiki/Sanford_Stadium "Sanford Stadium") in its first trip to the south. Wade's Alabama again won a national championship and [Rose Bowl](/wiki/1931_Rose_Bowl "1931 Rose Bowl") in 1930.

#### Coaches of the era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Coaches of the era")]

##### Glenn "Pop" Warner

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Glenn \"Pop\" Warner")]

Main article: [Pop Warner](/wiki/Pop_Warner "Pop Warner")

Glenn "Pop" Warner coached at several schools throughout his career, including the [University of Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_Bulldogs_football "Georgia Bulldogs football"), [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_Big_Red_football "Cornell Big Red football"), [University of Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Panthers_football "Pittsburgh Panthers football"), [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal_football "Stanford Cardinal football"), [Iowa State University](/wiki/Iowa_State_Cyclones_football "Iowa State Cyclones football"), and [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_Owls_football "Temple Owls football").[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett197620–21-114) One of his most famous stints was at the [Carlisle Indian Industrial School](/wiki/Carlisle_Indian_Industrial_School "Carlisle Indian Industrial School"), where he coached [Jim Thorpe](/wiki/Jim_Thorpe "Jim Thorpe"), who went on to become the first president of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), an [Olympic Gold Medalist](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics"), and is widely considered one of the best overall athletes in history.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVancil200020-116) Warner wrote one of the first important books of football strategy, *Football for Coaches and Players*, published in 1927.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Though the shift was invented by Stagg, Warner's [single wing](/wiki/Single_wing "Single wing") and double wing [formations](/wiki/Formation_(American_football) "Formation (American football)") greatly improved upon it; for almost 40 years, these were among the most important formations in football. As part of his single and double wing formations, Warner was one of the first coaches to effectively use the forward pass. Among his other innovations are modern blocking schemes, the [three-point stance](/wiki/Three-point_stance "Three-point stance"), and the [reverse](/wiki/Reverse_(American_football) "Reverse (American football)") play.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBennett197620–21-114) The youth football league, [Pop Warner Little Scholars](/wiki/Pop_Warner_Little_Scholars "Pop Warner Little Scholars"), was named in his honor.

##### Knute Rockne

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=College_football&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Knute Rockne")]

Main article: [Knute Rockne](/wiki/Knute_Rockne "Knute Rockne")

[Knute Rockne](/wiki/Knute_Rockne "Knute Rockne") rose to prominence in 1913 as an [end](/wiki/End_(American_football) "End (American football)") for the [University of Notre Dame](/wiki/University_of_Notre_Dame "University of Notre Dame"), then a largely unknown Midwestern Catholic school. When Army scheduled Notre Dame as a warm-up game, they thought little of the small school. Rockne and quarterback [Gus Dorais](/wiki/Gus_Dorais "Gus Dorais") made innovative use of the forward pass, still at that point a relatively unused weapon, to defeat Army 35–13 and helped establish the school as a national power. Rockne returned to coach the team in 1918, and devised the powerful [Notre Dame Box](/wiki/Notre_Dame_Box "Notre Dame Box") offense, based on Warner's single wing. He is credited with being the first major coach to emphasize offense over defense. Rockne is also credited with popularizing and perfecting the forward pass, a seldom used play at the time.[[118]](#cite_note-rockne-118) The [1924 team](/wiki/1924_Notre_Dame_Fighting_Irish_football_team "1924 Notre Dame Fighting Irish football team") featured the [Four Horsemen](/wiki/Four_Horsemen_(American_football) "Four Horsemen (American football)") backfield. In 1927, his complex shifts led directly to a rule change whereby all offensive players had to stop for a full second before the ball could be snapped. Rather than simply a regional team, Rockne's "Fighting Irish" became famous for [barnstorming](/wiki/Barnstorm_(athletics) "Barnstorm (athletics)") and played any team at any location. It was during Rockne's tenure that the annual [Notre Dame-University of Southern California rivalry](/wiki/Jeweled_Shillelagh "Jeweled Shillelagh") began.
